identity.title: Promoters of Performing Arts, Sports, and Similar Events with Facilities

lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market operators that repeatedly manage and staff arenas, stadiums, theaters, music halls, fairgrounds, and similar facilities for external promoters, event organizers, sponsors, renters, and attendees, including recurring venue booking, ticketing, marketing, event settlement, and facility operations.
lens.excluded_activities: One-off project entities, passive real-estate ownership, public or captive facilities without an outsourced customer relationship, sports teams and performing-arts companies classified elsewhere, promoters without facilities, and food-and-beverage operations that are not ancillary to venue management.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers include promoters, artists, corporate and private renters, sponsors, municipalities, and event attendees. Revenue commonly combines management or rental fees with tickets and ticketing fees, sponsorship, premium space, parking, merchandise commissions, food and beverage, and event-profit shares.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code combines self-promoted venue economics with repeat outsourced facility management. The lens retains only operators with repeat external-customer service relationships, because self-promoted events alone do not constitute a recurring outsourced service.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment and wages for combined NAICS 711300, not 711310, while ILO measures task exposure by broad occupation rather than this industry. I mapped higher exposure to clerical, finance, marketing, writing, customer-service, and planning tasks and low exposure to security, food service, maintenance, technical production, and in-person guest work, then judgmentally wage-weighted the BLS mix. Combining facilities with 711320 likely understates physical work and therefore biases exposure upward for 711310.
a.rationale: The BLS mix is broad: management is 10.79% of employment, business and financial operations 9.77%, office support 8.95%, and sales 5.35%, but protective services are 7.52%, personal care/service 16.45%, and substantial technical, maintenance, food-service, and event-floor work is physical. AI can substitute parts of campaign creation, scheduling, proposals, customer responses, bookkeeping, reporting, and event documentation, but not most live safety, crowd, facility, hospitality, or production execution.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task inventory from 15-25 independent 711310 venue operators, with current automation status and time-on-task by occupation, would materially change the estimate; vendor telemetry from venue CRM, booking, ticketing, and event-management systems would provide a useful cross-check.
caveats: The occupation table is for combined NAICS 711300 and includes promoters without facilities.
ILO exposure is global and occupation-level, and exposure does not imply feasible substitution.
OEWS employment shares include employers of all sizes rather than the lower-middle-market lens.

rho.bridge: Census BTOS measures current AI use across all U.S. nonfarm employer businesses and functions, not the fraction of exposed venue work implemented within five years. I use its 18% firm adoption, 32% employment-weighted adoption, limited functional breadth, and rare reported employment decreases as an adoption anchor, then allow five years for diffusion while discounting for small-firm systems integration, seasonal workflows, safety controls, data fragmentation, and human approval. Broader-economy adoption may overstate implementation in operationally physical venues.
rho.rationale: AI adoption is already meaningful but usually narrow and augmentative: Census reports 18% of firms using AI, 32% on an employment-weighted basis, 57% of adopters using it in no more than three functions, 66% relying solely on augmentation, and employment decreases at only 2% of firms. Venue operators can deploy proven tools in marketing, customer service, scheduling support, reconciliation, and document workflows, but realized labor avoidance will trail technical exposure because live-event errors are time-critical and fragmented legacy systems and variable labor complicate redesign.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal benchmark from venue-management software providers showing hours, error rates, staffing, and workflow adoption before and after AI deployment, segmented by facility size and function, would materially move this estimate.
caveats: BTOS is economy-wide and its AI question changed in late 2025 to cover any business function.
Current use is not the same as implemented labor substitution or avoidable hiring.
Public-assembly safety and contractual approval requirements vary by venue and municipality.

e.rationale: The official definition expressly includes both self-promotion in operated facilities and managing/staffing facilities for rent to other promoters. The latter is directly eligible, and recurring rentals, sponsorship service, ticketing, and operational management can make some mixed operators eligible. The estimate excludes firms whose economics are mainly proprietary event risk, restaurants, captive/public operation, or passive facility ownership. A public venue operator filing illustrates the heterogeneity: 2024 restaurant operations were 61% of revenue, event operations 30%, and amphitheater operations 9%.
e.change_evidence: Census product-line receipts or a verified firm-level sample of 711310 businesses classified by third-party management/rental revenue, self-promoted-event revenue, public/captive status, and EBITDA would materially change eligibility.
caveats: No source measures the eligible share of firms in the specified EBITDA band.
One public operator's revenue mix is illustrative and not representative of LMM firms.
A firm may mix eligible outsourced contracts with ineligible proprietary events.

s5.bridge: Gallup surveyed U.S. small-business owners across industries, not eligible 711310 LMM firms, and found that 17% of owners age 55 or older planned a sale, gift, or other ownership transfer within five years. I use that as an upper-middle anchor, include other qualifying transfers and deal-driven sales, and discount for municipal ties, facility leases, licenses, owner dependence, and mixed real-estate structures that can prevent a transferable control transaction. The broad small-business sample likely includes smaller firms and therefore may understate formal LMM deal activity, while its stated plans may overstate completed transfers.
s5.rationale: A five-year control-transfer probability in the mid-teens is consistent with broad owner intentions, but venue operations bring transfer friction: operating rights, leases, permits, public-private agreements, naming rights, promoter relationships, and facility assets may sit in different entities. Only completed control transfers of viable eligible operations count.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census from business brokers, Axial, PitchBook, and venue-management advisers matched to 711310 firms, plus owner-age and succession-plan interviews among eligible operators, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Gallup measures stated plans, not completed qualifying transfers.
The survey is cross-industry and not restricted to the EBITDA band.
Asset sales, venue closures, and internal reorganizations must be excluded.

q.rationale: Operators have multiple retention channels: fixed rental and management fees, ticketing/facility fees, sponsorship, parking, premium spaces, and food-and-beverage margin. IAVM recommends net-revenue metrics and event-level profit measurement, while an operator filing shows that venue profits may be contractually split after artist, security, utility, insurance, and other costs. AI benefits in internal overhead can initially be retained, but competitively bid management contracts, promoter profit shares, renter renewals, and transparent per-ticket charges should pass part of recurring savings to counterparties over five years.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level cohort analysis of management agreements, rental rate cards, event settlements, renewal repricing, and margins before and after workflow automation across at least 20 LMM operators would materially change retention.
caveats: No source isolates AI-created gross benefit or its contractual pass-through.
Retention differs sharply between fixed-fee management, rental, self-promotion, and profit-share arrangements.
Ancillary consumer revenue is demand-sensitive and should not be confused with retained cost savings.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for combined promoters and agents (NAICS 7113-4) to rise from $83.4 billion in 2024 to $103.6 billion in 2034, a 2.2% annual rate. Applying that constant annual rate for five years gives approximately 1.11. The aggregate includes 711320 and agents/managers and measures output rather than constant-quality quantity; venue-capacity constraints could make it overstate 711310 quantity, while facility utilization growth could make it relevant.
d5.rationale: The central case follows the official real-output trajectory and implies modest expansion in live-event and related demand. The range allows discretionary-spending weakness, event volatility, and venue saturation on the downside, and stronger utilization, sponsorship, and in-person experience demand on the upside.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year same-venue panel from IAVM, Pollstar, and venue operators covering event count, paid attendance, dark days, real rental rates, and constant-price ancillary volume specifically for independent 711310 facilities would materially change the demand ratio.
caveats: The BLS projection is broader than 711310 and spans ten years rather than five.
Real output is not a pure constant-quality service-quantity measure.
Live events are discretionary and exposed to recessions, artist supply, weather, and local competition.

o.rationale: Most surviving demand still requires an accountable operator because venues must coordinate physical access, capacity, emergency and security staffing, technical production, cleaning, maintenance, food service, permits, settlement, and real-time exception handling. BLS nevertheless expects contactless ticketing and check-in to reduce the industry's use of ushers and ticket takers, and some customer service, sales, and booking steps can become self-service. Those changes shrink labor per event but rarely eliminate the venue operator.
o.change_evidence: Observed staffing and responsibility maps from highly automated venues, including autonomous entry, concessions, customer service, scheduling, security escalation, and settlement, compared with conventional venues over multiple event types would materially change this estimate.
caveats: This is a judgment about operator necessity, not a measured automation statistic.
Large standardized arenas may automate more than small heterogeneous theaters and fairgrounds.
Regulation and insurer requirements can preserve human accountability even when tasks are automated.

narrative.executive_view: The coherent opportunity is repeat third-party venue management and operation, not proprietary event speculation. AI can reduce administrative and commercial workload around the event, while the physical facility and public-assembly obligations preserve the need for an operator. The code is heterogeneous, and eligibility is the largest gating judgment.
narrative.ai_impact: The most implementable uses are campaign and proposal drafting, CRM follow-up, customer-service triage, booking support, schedule and staffing suggestions, invoice and settlement reconciliation, contract extraction, reporting, and knowledge retrieval. Contactless entry and self-service continue to reduce front-of-house tasks. Security, maintenance, production, hospitality, crowd management, and live exception handling remain much less substitutable.
narrative.value_capture: Savings in internal overhead can be retained initially, especially where revenue is fixed rental, ticketing, sponsorship, parking, or premium-space income. Over five years, competitive bids, promoter profit shares, and renewal negotiations should transfer a meaningful portion to customers and partners. Multi-stream venue economics provide more levers than a pure hourly model but also make attribution difficult.
narrative.firm_availability: The supplied LMM firm count covers the code, but only a portion has the recurring external-service model. Ownership transfer is plausible in the mid-teens over five years, yet leases, municipal agreements, permits, real estate, and operator relationships can make control transfer more complex than an ordinary service company sale.
narrative.demand_durability: Official projections support modest real growth in the broader promoter-and-agent market. Physical attendance, venue utilization, sponsorship, and ancillary purchases sustain demand for operators even as ticketing and customer interfaces digitize. Downside risk remains high because events are discretionary and local facilities have fixed capacity and cost.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The principal research weakness is the lack of six-digit, LMM-specific evidence for outsourced-service eligibility, task mix, transactions, and contract pass-through. A bad case is a capital-heavy, owner-dependent operator whose revenue is mainly self-promoted events or restaurants, with municipal restrictions and little transferable management income. AI may improve service quality without reducing headcount, especially where labor is event-variable or safety-critical.
narrative.principal_driver: AI-addressable planning, marketing, customer-service, and settlement work sits around a durable physical venue operation with multiple revenue streams.
narrative.principal_weakness: NAICS 711310 mixes repeat outsourced facility management with proprietary promotion and ancillary hospitality, leaving eligibility and transferability poorly observed.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 711300
sources.supports: Combined-industry employment and wage mix: 163,640 workers; management 10.79%, business/financial 9.77%, arts/media 10.97%, protective service 7.52%, personal care/service 16.45%, sales 5.35%, and office support 8.95%, plus detailed occupations.

sources.title: Generative AI and Jobs: A global analysis of potential effects on job quantity and quality
sources.supports: Task-level occupational exposure evidence: clerical work is most exposed, with 24% of clerical tasks highly exposed and another 58% medium-exposed; most other broad groups have much lower high exposure, and augmentation is more likely than wholesale automation.

sources.title: The Microstructure of AI Diffusion: Evidence from Firms, Business Functions, and Worker Tasks
sources.supports: Nationally representative BTOS evidence: 18% of firms used AI in a business function and 32% employment-weighted; 57% of users deployed in three or fewer functions, 66% used AI solely to augment tasks, and only 2% reported AI-related employment decreases.

sources.title: NAICS 711310 Definition: Promoters of Performing Arts, Sports, and Similar Events with Facilities
sources.supports: Official scope includes organizing/promoting/managing events in facilities the establishment operates and managing and staffing arenas, stadiums, theaters, or related facilities for rent to other promoters; it distinguishes promoters without facilities.

sources.title: Arenas Performance Reporting Framework: An IAVM Handbook for Arenas Professionals
sources.supports: Industry operating model and metrics: venue revenue includes tickets, rentals, merchandise, parking, sponsorship, food and beverage, facility/ticketing fees, premium spaces and reimbursed labor; operators track net event income, per-cap revenue, attendance, capacity, expenses and dark-day costs.

sources.title: Venu Holding Corporation 2024 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Observed venue business-model example: rentals and event staffing, tickets/fees, naming rights, sponsorship, food and beverage, and profit-sharing contracts; 2024 revenue mix of 61% restaurant, 30% event operations and 9% amphitheater operations; permits and public-private restrictions illustrate transfer and operating constraints.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Fall 2024 U.S. small-business-owner survey: 17% of owners age 55 or older reported plans within five years to sell, gift, or otherwise transfer ownership, compared with 10% of younger owners.

sources.title: Employment and Output by Industry, 2024-2034
sources.supports: BLS projects real output for promoters of performing arts and sports and agents for public figures (7113-4) from $83.4 billion in 2024 to $103.6 billion in 2034, a 2.2% compound annual increase; 711300 employment is projected to grow 6.8%.

sources.title: Factors Affecting Occupational Utilization, Projected 2024-34
sources.supports: BLS expects the share of ushers, lobby attendants, and ticket takers in NAICS 711300 to decrease as contactless ticketing and check-in improve productivity.
